title: Oil spikes to $119 as Middle East conflict disables 17% of Qatar LNG capacity
content: Global energy markets were upended this week following retaliatory strikes on critical infrastructure in the Persian Gulf. After US-Israeli operations in Iran, strikes on the South Pars gas field and Qatar’s largest LNG plant have disabled approximately 17% of Qatar’s export capacity, with repairs expected to take years. Brent crude peaked at $119 per barrel before settling near $112, while European natural gas prices soared. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has halted 20% of global oil supply, sparking fears of a sustained inflationary shock. Equities retreated globally, with the S&P 500 closing below its 200-day moving average for the first time in 200 sessions, as investors rotated aggressively into the energy sector (+32% YTD) and safe-haven assets.

title: OECD slashes UK growth forecasts to 0.7% amid global energy price shock
content: In its Interim Economic Outlook released on 26 March, the OECD warned that the Middle East conflict has derailed the global recovery. The UK faced the sharpest downgrade among G20 nations, with 2026 GDP growth slashed to 0.7% from 1.2% previously. The report highlights that renewed energy spikes have revived inflation risks just as central banks were preparing to pivot. The OECD held its global growth forecast at 2.9%, citing AI-driven investment as a partial offset to the energy crisis, but warned of 'extensive repricing' if disruptions persist. Market reaction saw Bund and Treasury yields push to new cycle highs as traders priced in a 'higher-for-longer' regime, with the Germany-France 10-year spread widening to 71bps on fears of European fiscal strain.

title: Fed and BoE pause rate cuts as 'stagflation' fears grip bond markets
content: The Fed and BoE both held interest rates steady this week (3.50%-3.75% and 3.75% respectively), citing a 'wait-and-see' stance as energy-led inflation stalls progress. Fed Chair Powell acknowledged that core PCE is now projected at 2.7% for 2026, well above the 2% target, while the BoE's split 5-4 vote reflected growing internal friction. Markets have effectively priced out near-term easing, with UST 2-year yields jumping to 3.93% and 10-year yields hitting 4.40%, the highest since last July. The combination of sticky US PPI (0.7%) and surging crude has fueled 'stagflation' narratives, leading to a 7% stabilization-drop in the S&P 500 from its peak. Analysts suggest the 'policy cushion' to absorb further shocks is now critically thin.
